---
title: "Session 1 — Exercise"
subtitle: "Setting Up a Reproducible Workflow"
date: "12 March 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-tools: true
---

## Your task today

**Scenario:** You are a junior analyst at an international consultancy. Before
writing any analysis, your team requires all reports to follow a standard
document structure — a proper YAML header, numbered sections, and a bibliography.
Your task today is to build that structure from scratch.

You are **not** analysing data yet. You are building the container that will
hold your analysis for the rest of the semester.

Work through the six tasks below. Each task has a **check** — a short
description of what you should see when you have done it correctly.

---

## Task 1: Project setup

Set up a clean R project with the standard folder structure.

**Steps:**

1. In RStudio, go to **File → New Project → New Directory → New Project**
2. Give it a meaningful name (e.g. `my-analysis`) and choose a sensible location
3. Once the project opens, create three folders inside it:
   - `data/` — for raw data files
   - `figures/` — for exported plots
   - `output/` — for rendered reports
4. Create a new Quarto document: **File → New File → Quarto Document**
   Save it as `report.qmd` in your project root

::: {.callout-tip}
**How to create folders:** In the RStudio Files pane (bottom right), click
**New Folder**.
:::

**Check:** Your Files pane should show:

```
my-analysis/
├── my-analysis.Rproj
├── report.qmd
├── data/
├── figures/
└── output/
```

---

## Task 2: YAML front matter

Replace the default YAML header in `report.qmd` with a complete one.

**Steps:**

Delete whatever Quarto put in the YAML by default and replace it with this —
filling in your own title and name:

```yaml
---
title: "Your Report Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---
```

**Check:** The document has a YAML block between `---` markers at the top.
If you try to render now, it will warn about a missing `references.bib` — that
is expected and fixed in Task 4.

::: {.callout-note}
`date: today` tells Quarto to use today's date automatically every time the
document renders — no manual updating needed.
:::

---

## Task 3: Document structure

Add meaningful content sections to your report.

**Steps:**

Below the YAML, write at least **three sections** using Markdown headings.
Choose a topic relevant to international business — it does not need to be
sophisticated. For example:

```markdown
## Introduction

This report examines ... [one or two sentences].

## Data

The analysis uses data from ... [one or two sentences].

## Results

The main finding is ... [one or two sentences].
```

**Check:** When you render to HTML (`Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K`), you should see:

- A numbered table of contents on the left
- Three numbered sections in the document body

---

## Task 4: Bibliography

Add citations to your report using BibTeX.

**Steps:**

1. Copy the file `references.bib` from the session folder into your project
   folder (next to `report.qmd`)
2. In your document text, add at least two citations using `@key` syntax.
   The keys available in the provided file are:
   - `@BekezKezdi2021` — the main course textbook
   - `@Wooldridge2020` — an econometrics reference
   - `@Preston1975` — a classic applied paper
3. At the end of your document, add a `## References` heading (Quarto fills
   this in automatically)
4. Render to HTML and confirm the reference list appears

**Example citation in text:**

```markdown
As discussed in @BekezKezdi2021, regression analysis requires ...

For a formal treatment, see @Wooldridge2020.

## References
```

**Check:** The rendered HTML shows in-text citations (e.g. "Békés & Kézdi,
2021") and a formatted reference list at the end.

::: {.callout-note}
**Zotero integration** (optional): Zotero can generate `.bib` entries
automatically and sync with Quarto. This is covered in an optional tutorial
after the session — not required today.
:::

---

## Task 5: R code chunk

Add a working R code chunk that produces a plot.

**Steps:**

1. Insert a code chunk: **Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + I** (or use the Insert menu)
2. In the chunk, load the tidyverse and make any simple plot using built-in data

A minimal example to get started:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "A simple example plot."

library(tidyverse)

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "Engine displacement (litres)",
    y = "Highway fuel economy (mpg)"
  )
```

You can use any built-in dataset and any `ggplot2` plot you like — this is just
to confirm the workflow runs end to end.

**Check:** When you render, a figure appears in the HTML output with a caption.

---

## Task 6: Render to both formats

Confirm your document renders cleanly to both HTML and Word.

**Steps:**

1. Render to HTML: **Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K** (or click the Render button)
   The output appears in the Viewer pane or opens in your browser
2. Render to Word: in the Render button dropdown, select **docx**
   (or add `format: docx` temporarily and re-render)

**Check:** Both files exist in your project:

- `report.html` — opens in a browser, shows TOC, numbered sections, figure, references
- `report.docx` — opens in Word with the same structure

::: {.callout-tip}
The `.docx` output may look slightly different from the HTML — that is normal.
Word rendering is polished in Session 8.
:::

---

## Finished early?

- Try adding a second code chunk with a different plot or a summary table
- Explore YAML options: what does `code-fold: true` do? What about
  `theme: cosmo`?
- Read the [Quarto documentation on HTML documents](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html)
